summary:
The Valley of the Kings, a dry desert ravine on the west bank of the Nile opposite Thebes (modern Luxor), served as the primary royal necropolis of [[event:60|New Kingdom Egypt]] from approximately 1539 to 1075 BC. During this period, pharaohs and high officials were buried in elaborate rock-cut tombs beneath the cliffs of the valley, their mummies protected by layers of funerary equipment, magical texts, and concealment from grave robbers.

The decision to move royal burials from the visible pyramid tombs of earlier periods to hidden underground chambers reflected a recognition that the great pyramids, despite their size, had failed to protect royal mummies from plunder. The New Kingdom burial strategy combined geographic isolation, complex tomb layouts, and concealed entrances to thwart robbers — a strategy ultimately unsuccessful, as most tombs were violated in antiquity, some within decades of the burial.

The valley contains over sixty tombs, including those of [[event:1807|Ramesses II]], [[event:1805|Akhenaten]]'s father Amenhotep III, Seti I (whose tomb is among the most elaborately decorated), and most famously [[event:1806|Tutankhamun]], whose nearly intact tomb was discovered by Howard Carter in 1922. The tomb walls are covered with extracts from religious texts such as the [[event:1812|Book of the Dead]], the Book of the Hidden Chamber (Amduat), and the Book of Gates, providing detailed maps of the underworld through which the deceased king was expected to journey.

The Valley of the Kings is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the most intensively studied archaeological sites in the world. Modern excavations continue to reveal new chambers and tombs, and the application of ground-penetrating radar and other non-invasive technologies has suggested additional buried structures may remain undiscovered.
